coredata.dc:title: Hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots from biowaste using dwarf banana peel for environmental and biological applications
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Perumal, Suguna
Muthuchamy, Nallal
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: Hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots (HN-CDs) were produced by a simple hydrothermal method from biowaste, dwarf banana peel. XRD, Raman spectroscopy, and HRTEM analyses confirmed the graphitic structure and narrow size distribution of the HN-CDs. Nitrogen-doping to the carbon structure/framework and owing rich hydrophilic groups on HN-CDs surface were confirmed by XPS and ATR-FTIR spectroscopy techniques. HN-CDs emitted strong and tunable fluorescence (FL) and possessed a good quantum yield (23%). Thus this novel HN-CDs applied as a nanoprobe for the detection of metal ion (Fe3+ ion) in aqueous solution by the fluorometric method. Excessive or lesser amounts of Fe3+ ion in human bodies lead to diverse diseases. Additionally, the accumulation of Fe3+ ion in the environment will escort to a great threat. The prepared HN-CDs selectively and sensitively detect Fe3+ ions by the FL quenching of HN-CDs with a limit of detection of 0.66 μM in the range of 5–25 μM. Further, the HN-CDs employed as a biocompatible probe for the multicolor bioimaging in rat liver cells. Also, HN-CDs used as fluorescent ink for drawing and writing. Thus, the biowaste/biomass successfully turned to a useful nanoprobe for environmental protection and health care.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biowaste
Hydrothermal method
N-doped carbon dots
Tunable emission
Ferric ion detection
Bioimaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology ( 2012M3A7B4049675 ), the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) of Korea government ( 2017R1C1B5076345 ) and the MSIT of Korea government ( 2018R1A2B2004432 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectrum of synthesized HN-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of synthesized HN-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s regions of synthesized HN-CDs.
(a-c) HRTEM images and (d) particle size distribution graph of synthesized HN-CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorbance spectrum and (b) FL excitation spectrum of synthesized HN-CDs. (c) excitation-dependent FL emission spectra and (d) the corresponding normalized spectra of synthesized HN-CDs.
FL emission spectra of synthesized HN-CDs under different irradiation time. Inset: Naked eye view of digital photographs of HN-CDs solution at different irradiation times under the UV-light.

(a) FL emission spectra of synthesized HN-CDs with 1 mM of different ions and (b) corresponding FL quenching (selectivity) of Fe3+ ions compared to that of other metal ions. (c) The change in the FL emission of HN-CDs on the addition of Fe3+ ions and (d) the relationship between (F0-F/F0)*100 and Fe3+ concentrations (5–25 μM) and a fitting line for calculating the LOD.
Digital photographs of aqueous HN-CDs solutions with 1 mM of different metal ions under UV-light and daylight.
Schematic for the FL quenching of HN-CDs on the addition of Fe3+ ions and formation of HN-CDs-Fe3+ complex.
Naked eye vision of the handwritten fluorescent words using the aqueous solution of HN-CDs under daylight and UV-light.

Confocal microscopy FL image of Clone 9 hepatocytes (Rat river cells) incubated (different staining time of 12, 24 and 36 h) with HN-CDs under different excitation wavelengths (405, 488 and 555 nm) and corresponding bright-field images as well as merged images.
Illustration of the synthesis procedure of fluorescent HN-CDs from dwarf banana peel.
Comparison of excitation wavelength (λex) and quantum yield (QY) for different CDs/N-CDs with various carbon precursors.
Comparison of different CDs/N-CDs with various carbon precursors for the sensing of Fe3+ ions.

head.ce:title: Hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots from biowaste using dwarf banana peel for environmental and biological applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Pusan National University, Busan 46241, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Pusan National University, Busan 46241, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots (HN-CDs) were produced by a simple hydrothermal method from biowaste, dwarf banana peel. XRD, Raman spectroscopy, and HRTEM analyses confirmed the graphitic structure and narrow size distribution of the HN-CDs. Nitrogen-doping to the carbon structure/framework and owing rich hydrophilic groups on HN-CDs surface were confirmed by XPS and ATR-FTIR spectroscopy techniques. HN-CDs emitted strong and tunable fluorescence (FL) and possessed a good quantum yield (23%). Thus this novel HN-CDs applied as a nanoprobe for the detection of metal ion (Fe3+ ion) in aqueous solution by the fluorometric method. Excessive or lesser amounts of Fe3+ ion in human bodies lead to diverse diseases. Additionally, the accumulation of Fe3+ ion in the environment will escort to a great threat. The prepared HN-CDs selectively and sensitively detect Fe3+ ions by the FL quenching of HN-CDs with a limit of detection of 0.66 μM in the range of 5–25 μM. Further, the HN-CDs employed as a biocompatible probe for the multicolor bioimaging in rat liver cells. Also, HN-CDs used as fluorescent ink for drawing and writing. Thus, the biowaste/biomass successfully turned to a useful nanoprobe for environmental protection and health care.

ce:section.ce:para: The earth’s water resources such as rivers, lakes, and oceans were extensively polluted by household and industries wastes. Hence efficient treatment method to remove contaminants is urgently needed. Prevention is a very proficient technique to avoid the earth’s water resources pollution. Thus the usage of wastes especially biowaste is advisable. Recently, researchers focusing to use biowastes for the preparation of carbon-based materials and carbon dots (CDs) [1–3]. The CDs were used in various applications including medical diagnosis, photocatalysis, fluorescent sensing, optoelectronic devices, cellular labeling, and bioimaging because of their inimitable properties such as excellent optical properties, aqueous solubility, high chemical stability, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [4–11]. Generally, CDs were prepared by either “top-down” or “bottom-up” methods [12–14]. The former method includes laser ablation, chemical-oxidation, electrochemical-oxidation, hydrothermal treatment and so on [15–17]. Among these methods, hydrothermal treatment was used by many researchers for preparing CDs [18]. Recently doping of CDs by heteroatoms has been actively pursued. Among the heteroatoms, nitrogen-doping is extensively studied because of their unique properties. Nitrogen-doping CDs (N-CDs) showed excellent optical properties including good fluorescence (FL) with high quantum yield [19,20]. Thus, doped CDs can be used for various applications, for example, the recognition of various metal ions, bioimaging, and biosensors. Recently ecofriendly preparations of heteroatoms-doped CDs were extensively studied [21,22]. We published the synthesis of heteroatom-doped CDs from leaves, flowers, fruits, and the prepared CDs were successfully used for bioimaging, sensing and fluorescent ink applications. N-CDs were synthesized from Chionanthus retusus and Phyllanthus acidus fruit extract was employed for the determination of metal ion and biological applications [23]. Synthesized N-CDs from Magnolia liliiflora flowers were used for sensing of Fe3+, cellular multicolor FL imaging, and fluorescent ink [19]. Fluorescent N-CDs using Phyllanthus emblica juice was used as a perfect multicolor fluorescent candidate for biomedical and as fluorescent ink applications [24], and N-CDs derived from betel leaves used in an environmental and in biological applications [20].

As a continuation, herein we describe a facile and an efficient pathway to attain hydrophilic nitrogen-doped carbon dots (HN-CDs) with high quantum yield (QY = ~23%) by hydrothermal method. Importantly, the HN-CDs were prepared using a waste banana peel. The optical properties and functional groups on HN-CDs surface were thoroughly studied using UV–Vis, FL studies, ATR-FTIR spectroscopy, and XPS measurements. HN-CDs showed excellent FL property with rich functional groups and thus HN-CDs used as a fluorescent probe for the determination of metal ions. Fe3+ ion was detected efficiently among the other metal ions. Further, it showed very good biocompatibility and stable FL, thus it used in bioimaging applications and used as a modern fluorescent ink.

HN-CDs were synthesized using simple hydrothermal-carbonization treatment of dwarf banana peel. Briefly, the dwarf banana peel was ground well with deionized water. The suspension of dwarf banana peel (70 mL) and aqueous ammonia (1 mL) were taken into a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and then hydrothermally heated at 200 °C for a period of 24 h. The autoclave was cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, the resulting solution was passed through a mixed cellulose membrane with pore size 0.22 μm to remove aggregated particles/dust particles. This gives a clear golden brownish solution. The obtained HN-CDs were kept in a refrigerator at 10 °C for additional measurements. Scheme 1 shows an illustration of the synthesis procedure of HN-CDs from the dwarf banana peel (biowaste).

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectrum of synthesized HN-CDs from the dwarf Banana peel is shown in Fig. 1 (a). The XRD spectrum reveals the presence of three dominant peaks around 22.8, 42.5 and 53.7° which are credited to the (0 0 2) (1 0 0) and (0 0 4) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials, respectively [25,26]. The peak positioned at 22.8° corresponding to the plane 100 with an interlayer spacing of ~0.37 nm. This 002 plane interlayer spacing is vaguely superior than that of typical bulk graphite (~0.34 nm). This confirms the synthesized HN-CDs predominantly with graphitic structure and has a small amount of amorphous carbon. The amorphous carbon is might be due to the surface functionalities of synthesized HN-CDs [19,27]. Further, the graphitization of synthesized HN-CDs was further affirmed by the Raman spectroscopy technique. The Raman spectrum in Fig. 1(b) displays two peaks around 1367 cm−1 and 1591 cm−1. The corresponding peaks referring to D-band responsible for the disordered surface and G-band attributed to the sp2 carbon networks from HN-CDs, respectively [28–30]. The intensity of D-band (ID) is lower than that of G-band (IG) suggest the good graphitization of synthesized HN-CDs [31,32]. Besides, the area of D-band (AD) and G-band (AG) represents the concentration of defects in the graphitic structure, and the lower AD in the Raman spectrum of HN-CDs (inset Fig. 1(b)) implies the integrity of the graphitic shells is sufficiently high to protect the core carbon material well from oxidation which resulting good graphitization [31,33]. Moreover, the broadness of Raman peaks supports the smaller size with a lesser thickness of the HN-CDs. ATR-FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2 ) showed to reveal the surface functionalization of HN-CDs. The synthesized HN-CDs possess broadband around 3400 cm−1 assigned to combined O–H and N–H stretching vibration bands [34]. The sp3 C–H stretching band observed around 2830 and 2950 cm−1 and C = O stretching vibration of the carboxylic acid appears around 1720 cm−1 [35]. The absorption peak around 1590 cm−1 indicates the presence of CC stretching vibration of aromatic rings and peak at 1450 cm−1 corresponds to C–N stretching vibration which indicates the presence of N element on the surface of HN-CDs [36,37]. Moreover, some peaks at 1250, 1180 and 1080 cm−1, reveals the presence of –COO, C–OH, and C–O–C functional groups, respectively [38–40]. The XRD, FTIR, and Raman reveal the existence of groups such as –COOH, –OH, –NH2, and aromatic rings in the HN-CDs.

Furthermore, the functionalities and elemental composition of synthesized HN-CDs were confirmed via the XPS analysis. The survey XPS spectrum presented in Fig. 3 (a) evident three distinguish peaks around 285, 400 and 532 eV, suggesting that the HN-CDs consisted of C, N, and O elements with atomic ratios as 75, 7, and 18%, respectively. This suggests the presence of great amounts of carbon (C) and oxygen (O) elements, and the acceptable amounts of nitrogen (N) elements on the surface of the HN-CDs. The corresponding binding energies were further confirmed by their high-resolution XPS spectra. In Fig. 3(b), high-resolution XPS of the C 1s band was deconvoluted into four peaks at 284 eV, 286 eV, and 288 eV which are assigned to CC/C–C, C–N/C–O, CO/CN and HO–CO bonds, respectively [41]. The high-resolution N 1s peaks of HN-CDs shows three peaks at 398, 399.5 and 400.5 eV attributed to C–N–C, C–N–H, and (C)3–N, respectively (Fig. 3(c)) [42,43]. The three fitted peaks at 531, 533 eV, and 533.5 eV in O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3(d)) can be ascribed to the presence of CO, C–OH/C–O–C, OC–OH groups, respectively [44,45]. The XPS analyses evidently signify the existence of oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups in the synthesized HN-CDs including −COOH, −OH, −C–N, and so on, which well agreed with the ATR-FTIR spectrum. The existence of these functionalities in the synthesized HN-CDs is responsible for their good dispersibility/soluble in water.

The morphologies of the HN-CDs were characterized by TEM. Fig. 4 (a-c) shows the HRTEM images of HN-CDs. It can be seen from Fig. 4 (a and b) that HN-CDs maintained spherical shapes and monodispersed without apparent aggregation. Fig. 4(c) reveals the good crystallinity of HN-CDs and forms a single contiguous crystalline phase with a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the lattice fringes of typical graphene facet (1 0 0) plane [46,47]. From the size, histograms are shown in Fig. 4(d), the particle sizes are mainly between 2.5 and 5.5 nm and the average diameter of HN-CDs is around 4 nm, determined by fitting a Gaussian model.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the HN-CDs. Two absorbance bands centered at 272 nm and 320 nm are clear from the UV–Vis absorption spectrum. The first peak around 272 nm attributed to the π-π* transitions of the aromatic CC (sp2) bond. The second absorption band at 320 nm corresponds to n-π* transitions of the CO/CN bond while the visible absorption band is attributed to the oxygen- and nitrogen-functional groups on the surface of HN-CDs [48–50]. The FL excitation spectrum of the HN-CDs is shown as Fig. 5(b), the maximum excitation wavelength observed at 345 nm. The high intense excitation peak of Fig. 5(b) confirms the bright FL of HN-CDs. FL emission was carried out at different excitation wavelengths to confirm the maximum FL (Fig. 5(c)). The FL intensity increased with an increase in excitation wavelength from 290 nm to 345 nm in excitation wavelength than the FL intensity was decreased gradually with an increase in excitation wavelength from 345 nm to 460 nm (Fig. 5(c) and Fig. S1). The maximum FL emission intensity was observed at an excitation wavelength of 445 nm. Besides, redshift in FL intensities was observed by an increase in excitation wavelength from 310 nm to 460 nm as shown in Fig. 5(d). When excited with 310 nm, a peak was observed at 395 nm which showed redshift with an increase in excitation wavelength. As a result, the emission peak at 505 nm was observed with an excitation wavelength of 460 nm. The surface functionalities of HN-CDs introduce trap states with different energy levels resulting in excitation-dependent emission [51]. The FL QY of HN-CDs was calculated to be ~23% with quinine sulfate (54%) as the reference [19]. Table 1 shows the comparison of QY for different CDs/N-CDs with various carbon precursors. As can be seen, the QY of 23% of this work auspiciously higher than the earlier reports, CDs/N-CDs [52–59]. The stability of synthesized HN-CDs is one of the crucial characteristics of real-time applications. The photobleaching and stability of HN-CDs were determined by irradiating HN-CDs aqueous solution under UV lamp. The aqueous solution was excited with 365 nm for 120 min with 30 min intervals. Fig. 6 reveals the invisible changes in FL intensities of HN-CDs with different time intervals. This confirms that the synthesized HN-CDs have very good stability. Further, the inset photographs prove the photostability of synthesized HN-CDs. The photographs of 0, 30, 60, 90, and 120 min irradiation of HN-CDs are almost alike with the bright blue color by naked eye.

It is very well known that environmental pollution with metal ions leads to a serious problem. So this issue needs urgent attention, recently CDs were used to detect metal ions. The detection of metal ions was done by FL quenching of CDs which takes place by charge transfer between metal ions and CDs. Thus in this work, we added 1 mM of metal ions Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ were added to the synthesized HN-CDs solution and measured the FL intensity. Only Fe3+ showed quenching of FL whereas all other metal ions retained its FL spectra as shown in Fig. 7 (a). The Fig. 7(b) shows FL quenching for metal ions selectivity. This suggests that using synthesized HN-CDs, Fe3+ ion can be easily detected due to the strong affinity between HN-CDs and Fe3+ ion [60]. The surface functionalities (−COOH, −OH and –C–NH) with Fe3+ ions might form a complex and this complex might efficiently avoid the hydrolyzing of Fe3+ in solution. This is responsible for the FL quenching of HN-CDs [60]. To check the quenching limitation of Fe3+ ion by NH-CDs, the quenching effects of Fe3+ ion on NH-CDs solution was checked by the addition of different concentrations between 5 and 25 μM (Fig. 7(c)). As the concentration of Fe3+ ion increases, the FL intensity decreased gradually however, change in wavelength is not observed. Fig. 7(d) represents the plot between the FL intensities and Fe3+ concentrations. The plot suggests an excellent linear association with linear correlation (R2 = 0.998). Limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.66 μM using the following equation (1). Table 2 displays the comparison of different CDs/N-CDs with various carbon precursors for sensing of Fe3+ ions. The LOD value (0.66 μM) of this result is favorably lower than the previous reported LOD values [61–68].
(1)
LOD =
3 σ
S
where σ is the standard deviation (n = 5) and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot.

Fig. 8 reveals the digital photographs of an aqueous solution of HN-CDs with 1 mM of different metal ions under UV-light and daylight. Under UV-light, the digital picture was obtained using a UV lamp with λex = 365 nm, which exposes the quenching of HN-CDs solution with Fe3+ ion. The other metal ion retains its FL. The daylight inspection too shows the FL quenching in the existence of Fe3+ ions. The plausible mechanism is shown as Fig. 9 it is the functional groups on HN-CDs such as –OH and –NH may interact with Fe3+ ion. The presence of hydrophilic functionalities such as hydroxyl, amine and carboxylic in HN-CDs was confirmed from XPS and FTIR spectroscopies. These functional groups could form a complex with the Fe3+ ions. Therefore, the electron/energy transfer (ET) between Fe3+ ions and HN-CDs (non-radiative energy/ET) takes place and thus the FL of HN-CDs is quenched [22]. The ATR-FTIR spectrum of HN-CDs after bonding with ferric ions (Fe3+) is shown in Fig. S2. The vibrational bands between 400 and 4000 cm−1 for the surface functional groups of HN-CDs exhibit apparent changes, which suggest that the surface functional groups of HN-CDs produce a new electronic effect due to the binding of Fe3+ ions. In particular, the absorption band intensities of O–H, N–H, –COO and C–OH cm−1 are considerably decreased for the HN-CDs + Fe3+ complex as compared to bare HN-CDs. Meanwhile, the absorption bands are shifted slightly towards higher wavenumber for the HN-CDs + Fe3+ complex. In particular, O–H and C–H vibrational bands are largely shifted. Importantly, the stretching vibrations of OC–OH (1720 cm−1) and C-NH (1450 cm−1) almost disappear after the mutual complexation of HN-CDs and Fe3+ ions, therefore the fluorescence decrement of HN-CDs is a reference to the synergism of O- and N-containing functional groups (OC–OH, C–OH, N–H and C–N) integrated with Fe3+ ions [7].

Since HN-CDs showed excellent dispersibility/solubility, strong FL, photobleaching resistance and transparency in the visible region, it can be used as fluorescent ink. The HN-CDs aqueous solution is colorless under daylight but shows bright blue FL under 365 nm UV lamp as shown in Fig. S3. It suggests that HN-CDs can be directly used as ink to write fluorescent words. For this, a commercially available sketch pen was filled with an aqueous solution of synthesized HN-CDs and used for writing on a normal filter paper. As shown in Fig. 10 , it is evident that the written words are visible under a UV light with excitation 365 nm. Under daylight, the handwritings are invisible. Interestingly the text (YEUNGNAM UNIVERSITY) remained constant even after 60 days (when stored under ambient conditions) (Fig. S4). This advises that the HN-CDs can be used as invisible ink for applications such that for secret communications and anticounterfeiting applications [69,70]. The high-quality fluorescent wording can be obtained by using HN-CDs fluorescent ink. Moreover, this fluorescent ink was biocompatible, permanent, clear, pollution-free, and easily washable which can replace the traditional fluorescent ink.

The cytotoxicity of the HN-CDs was examined using Clone 9 hepatocytes cells with an MTT viability assay (Fig. S5). The HN-CDs showed trivial toxicity to the Colon 9 hepatocytes cells and the relative cell viability was decreased from 100 to 98% with the change in concentration from 0 to 200 μg mL−1. The changes in cell viability even after 36 h exposure were negligible. Thus confirms the biocompatible nature of the synthesized HN-CDs. The localization of HN-CDs on Clone 9 hepatocytes has also been studied by using confocal-microscopy imaging. Fig. 11 shows the confocal-microscopy images of HN-CDs with Clone 9 hepatocytes cells at incubation periods of 12, 24, and 36 h. When HN-CDs excited 405, 488 and 555 nm, it emits red, green and blue emissions, respectively. No FL signals were observed for control samples, without HN-CDs. Whereas feeble and strong emission signals from the cytoplasm and nucleus, respectively were observed at 12 h incubation periods. After longer incubation 36 h time, strong FL was observed at the nucleus and cytoplasm. The brightness of the emission is almost the same for the incubation periods 12 and 24 h. Owing to the small size of HN-CDs, it entered the Clone 9 hepatocytes cells within 12 h. Increase in intensities with incubation times reflecting the amount of intracellular uptake of HN-CDs. This suggests that the synthesized HN-CDs will be a good candidate in biomedical applications as fluorescent indicators.

In summary, we have reported HN-CDs via a hydrothermal route from the banana peel (biowaste). The functional groups that exist in HN-CDs were confirmed using ATR-FTIR and XPS measurements. The Raman study confirms the graphitization of HN-CDs. HN-CDs possess uniform spherical morphology with 4.0 nm in size. HN-CDs exhibited excitation dependent FL emission with good QY (~23%) and the FL intensities were retained even after long time storage. HN-CDs employed as a fluorescent nanoprobe for metal ions detection. Especially HN-CDs detect Fe3+ ion efficiently with LOD of 0.66 μM. Because of its excellent FL property, HN-CDs was employed as fluorescent ink and for bioimaging applications. HN-CDs were internalized very well in Clone 9 hepatocytes and displayed the different FL color with the variation of excitation wavelengths. Besides, as a fluorescent ink, HN-CDs could be an ideal or alternative candidate for the commercial dyes. Overall, results suggest that the synthesized HN-CDs will be a potential candidate for environmental and commercial applications shortly.

Raji Atchudan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel Edison: Methodology, Resources, Validation. Suguna Perumal: Writing - review & editing. Nallal Muthuchamy: Formal analysis, Software. Yong Rok Lee: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675), the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) of Korea government (2017R1C1B5076345) and the MSIT of Korea government (2018R1A2B2004432).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
